stem:
A 31-year-old female accountant attends your general practice clinic asking about adult autism assessment. She describes lifelong exhaustion after social events, scripting conversations in advance, intense interest in spreadsheet design since childhood, and sensory aversion to fluorescent lighting and clothing tags. She has never had developmental concerns flagged at school because she achieved high marks and modelled her behaviour on a chosen peer.

She presents now after her recently diagnosed nephew prompted her to read about autistic women. She has well-treated generalised anxiety on sertraline 100 mg daily. She is articulate, makes brief eye contact, and her PHQ-9 today is 6.

data_table.Examination: Calm engaged manner; mild fidgeting with pen; no thought disorder
data_table.Investigations: AQ-10 score 8 of 10; RAADS-14 screen score 28 (high)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in her diagnostic pathway?

options.text: Refer to a multidisciplinary autism diagnostic service for full assessment including developmental history and standardised tools
options.rationale: The national guideline requires multidisciplinary assessment with developmental history, observational tools and impairment assessment; this is the appropriate next step after a positive screen with consistent lifelong features. (Source: National Guideline for the Assessment and Diagnosis of Autism in Australia 2018)
source_refs: National Guideline for the Assessment and Diagnosis of Autism in Australia 2018

options.text: Reassure her that autism is unlikely given her successful career and high educational attainment in adulthood
options.rationale: Autistic women frequently mask through high cognitive effort and present in adulthood when role demands exceed compensatory capacity; attainment does not exclude diagnosis. (Source: National Guideline for the Assessment and Diagnosis of Autism in Australia 2018)
source_refs: National Guideline for the Assessment and Diagnosis of Autism in Australia 2018

options.text: Request a single psychometric IQ assessment from a psychologist to clarify whether her presentation reflects autism
options.rationale: IQ testing alone cannot diagnose autism and is neither sensitive nor specific; multidisciplinary assessment integrating developmental, behavioural and functional information is required. (Source: National Guideline for the Assessment and Diagnosis of Autism in Australia 2018)
source_refs: National Guideline for the Assessment and Diagnosis of Autism in Australia 2018

options.text: Increase sertraline to 150 mg daily and review in eight weeks before reconsidering any neurodevelopmental assessment
options.rationale: There is no current evidence of inadequately treated anxiety with a PHQ-9 of 6, and dose-escalating sertraline addresses neither the screening result nor the lifelong sensory and social features described. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Psychotropic 2023)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Psychotropic 2023

options.text: Order brain MRI and quantitative EEG to look for organic correlates of her sensory and social difficulties first
options.rationale: Neuroimaging and qEEG have no established role in routine adult autism diagnosis and would delay appropriate multidisciplinary assessment without changing management. (Source: National Guideline for the Assessment and Diagnosis of Autism in Australia 2018)
source_refs: National Guideline for the Assessment and Diagnosis of Autism in Australia 2018

explanation.summary: Autism in women is frequently missed because of social camouflaging and internalising presentations, with diagnostic delay common until adulthood. The Australian national guideline requires multidisciplinary diagnostic assessment incorporating developmental history, standardised observational measures and functional impairment data. Positive screens such as the AQ-10 and RAADS-14 indicate the need for full assessment rather than confirming diagnosis. Educational attainment, eye contact and articulate speech do not exclude autism in women.
key_points: Autistic women often mask socially with high cognitive cost
AQ-10 and RAADS-14 are screens, not diagnostic instruments
National guideline requires multidisciplinary diagnostic assessment
High educational attainment does not exclude autism
explanation.pearls: When a woman over 25 presents with positive AQ-10 (>=6) plus lifelong sensory and social features consistent across settings, refer to a multidisciplinary autism diagnostic service rather than relying on a single discipline or screening tool alone.

sources.label: National Guideline for the Assessment and Diagnosis of Autism in Australia 2018

stem: A 26-year-old man is referred to your community mental health team after a third psychiatric admission in two years. During admissions he has presented with grandiose delusions, persecutory beliefs, decreased sleep and pressured speech responsive to olanzapine and lithium combination. Collateral history from his sister and chart review confirm that on two separate occasions between admissions, while euthymic with normal sleep, energy and goal-directed activity, he continued to hold fixed persecutory delusions for at least six weeks before any subsequent mood episode. He has no substance use disorder and his thyroid function is normal.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic mood today; no current psychotic symptoms; insight partial
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.8 mIU/L; urine drug screen negative on two occasions
data_table.Medications: Olanzapine 15 mg nocte; lithium 750 mg nocte (level 0.7 mmol/L)

lead_in: Which diagnosis best fits this longitudinal course?

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with mood-congruent psychotic features during manic episodes and full inter-episode remission
options.rationale: Bipolar I with psychotic features requires that psychotic symptoms occur only during mood episodes; persistent delusions outside mood episodes are incompatible with this diagnosis. (Source: DSM-5-TR Schizoaffective Disorder Criteria)
source_refs: DSM-5-TR Schizoaffective Disorder Criteria

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder bipolar type given delusions persisting for at least two weeks outside the mood episodes documented
options.rationale: DSM-5-TR schizoaffective disorder requires psychotic symptoms for at least two weeks without prominent mood symptoms in the lifetime course, alongside mood episodes meeting major mood disorder criteria; his six-week euthymic delusional periods satisfy this. (Source: DSM-5-TR Schizoaffective Disorder Criteria)
source_refs: DSM-5-TR Schizoaffective Disorder Criteria

options.text: Schizophrenia with comorbid major depressive episodes superimposed during periods of severe negative symptom burden
options.rationale: Schizophrenia diagnosis would require six months of continuous illness with negative symptoms or social decline; the longitudinal pattern of full mood episodes meeting bipolar criteria better fits schizoaffective bipolar type. (Source: DSM-5-TR Schizophrenia Criteria)
source_refs: DSM-5-TR Schizophrenia Criteria

options.text: Delusional disorder persecutory type with episodic comorbid bipolar I disorder requiring separate sequential treatment courses
options.rationale: Delusional disorder excludes major mood episodes that disproportionately exceed delusional periods and excludes any history of schizophrenia-spectrum positive symptoms during admissions. (Source: DSM-5-TR Delusional Disorder Criteria)
source_refs: DSM-5-TR Delusional Disorder Criteria

options.text: Substance-induced psychotic disorder despite two negative urine drug screens given the episodic nature of his presentations
options.rationale: Two negative urine drug screens with collateral confirmation of no substance use and persistent symptoms beyond any plausible intoxication or withdrawal window rule out a substance-induced psychotic disorder. (Source: DSM-5-TR Substance-Induced Psychotic Disorder Criteria)
source_refs: DSM-5-TR Substance-Induced Psychotic Disorder Criteria

explanation.summary: Schizoaffective disorder bipolar type requires both a major mood episode (manic for the bipolar type) concurrent with the active phase of schizophrenia and at least two weeks of psychotic symptoms in the absence of prominent mood symptoms in the lifetime course. This longitudinal feature is the discriminator from bipolar I with psychotic features, where psychosis is confined to mood episodes. Treatment combines antipsychotic and mood stabiliser. Distinguishing schizoaffective from bipolar with psychosis matters for prognosis and long-term medication strategy.
key_points: Schizoaffective requires >=2 weeks of psychosis without prominent mood symptoms
Schizoaffective bipolar type requires lifetime manic episodes
Bipolar I with psychotic features confines psychosis to mood episodes
Discriminator is longitudinal not cross-sectional
explanation.pearls: If a patient with documented manic episodes also has psychotic features that persist for >=2 weeks during euthymia, recode the diagnosis from bipolar I with psychotic features to schizoaffective disorder bipolar type and continue both mood stabiliser and antipsychotic indefinitely.

stem:
A 22-year-old apprentice mechanic is brought to your emergency department by his housemates after two days of paranoid beliefs that his employer is poisoning his food. He has been smoking high-potency cannabis daily for the past four months, with last use yesterday evening. He has no prior psychiatric admissions but reports brief paranoia after heavy use on three previous occasions, each lasting under 48 hours and resolving with sleep.

He has no first-degree family history of psychosis. He is alert and oriented, vital signs are stable, and his urine drug screen confirms cannabis without other substances detected.

data_table.Examination: Mildly agitated; pressured speech; persecutory delusional ideation
data_table.Investigations: UDS THC positive, methamphetamine and cocaine negative; ECG QTc 412 ms

lead_in: Which classification best fits his current presentation?

options.text: Cannabis intoxication only with paranoia expected to resolve once acute intoxication phase concludes within hours
options.rationale: Symptoms persisting for two days well beyond the typical intoxication window and meeting full delusional intensity exceed simple intoxication and warrant a psychotic disorder formulation. (Source: DSM-5-TR Substance-Induced Psychotic Disorder Criteria)
source_refs: DSM-5-TR Substance-Induced Psychotic Disorder Criteria

options.text: First-episode schizophrenia with cannabis use as an aggravating environmental risk factor in this young adult man
options.rationale: Schizophrenia requires six months of continuous illness with one month of active-phase symptoms; a 48-hour presentation in active substance use does not yet satisfy this. (Source: DSM-5-TR Schizophrenia Criteria)
source_refs: DSM-5-TR Schizophrenia Criteria

options.text: Cannabis-induced psychotic disorder given two days of delusional symptoms developing during or soon after cannabis use
options.rationale: DSM-5-TR cannabis-induced psychotic disorder applies when prominent delusions or hallucinations develop during or soon after substance use, exceed expected intoxication and last beyond the acute intoxication window. (Source: DSM-5-TR Substance-Induced Psychotic Disorder Criteria)
source_refs: DSM-5-TR Substance-Induced Psychotic Disorder Criteria

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder with marked stressor given recent workplace conflict precipitating the persecutory belief content
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder requires that symptoms are not better explained by substance use; the temporal association with daily cannabis use and positive UDS makes substance-induced the better fit. (Source: DSM-5-TR Brief Psychotic Disorder Criteria)
source_refs: DSM-5-TR Brief Psychotic Disorder Criteria

options.text: Delusional disorder persecutory type with substance use as an incidental finding unrelated to the persecutory delusion
options.rationale: Delusional disorder requires symptoms for at least one month and exclusion of substance-attributable psychosis; daily cannabis use with active intoxication makes this diagnosis inappropriate at presentation. (Source: DSM-5-TR Delusional Disorder Criteria)
source_refs: DSM-5-TR Delusional Disorder Criteria

explanation.summary: Cannabis-induced psychotic disorder is the appropriate cross-sectional diagnosis when delusions or hallucinations develop during or soon after cannabis use and exceed the expected intoxication picture. Diagnosis requires reassessment after a sustained abstinence period (typically a month) because persistent symptoms beyond expected substance effects reclassify the presentation as a primary psychotic disorder. High-potency cannabis use significantly increases the risk of subsequent schizophrenia-spectrum disorder. Acute management is supportive with short-term low-dose antipsychotic if needed.
key_points: Substance-induced psychotic disorder requires symptoms exceeding expected intoxication
Diagnosis is provisional pending observation through abstinence
Persistence beyond a month off cannabis reclassifies as primary psychotic disorder
High-potency cannabis confers a meaningful schizophrenia risk
explanation.pearls: If psychotic symptoms persist for >1 month after verified cannabis abstinence, recode from cannabis-induced psychotic disorder to schizophreniform or schizophrenia and offer continuing antipsychotic plus structured substance-use psychosocial intervention.

subtopic_detail: BPD inpatient splitting and DBT principles
stem: You are the registrar covering an inpatient psychiatric unit overnight. A 24-year-old woman with borderline personality disorder admitted three days ago following a low-lethality overdose tells you the day team has been cruel to her and that you are the only doctor who has ever understood her. She asks you to immediately rewrite her discharge plan, prescribe diazepam at night and grant unescorted leave tomorrow, threatening that she will discharge against medical advice and harm herself if you do not. She has a structured DBT-informed care plan from her treating community team.

data_table.Examination: Tearful but engaged; no acute intoxication; mental state otherwise unchanged
data_table.Investigations: Bloods normal on admission; no current ECG concerns
data_table.Medications: Sertraline 150 mg daily; no benzodiazepines on regular chart

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate response?

options.text: Schedule her under the Mental Health Act tonight to prevent the threatened self-discharge and remove decisional autonomy
options.rationale: Mental Health Act scheduling for contingency-driven threats in BPD without an acute risk increment or treatable mental illness is generally inappropriate and may worsen long-term outcomes. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Borderline Personality Disorder 2012)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Borderline Personality Disorder 2012

options.text: Prescribe diazepam 5 mg nocte tonight as a once-only dose to settle her distress and prevent self-discharge tonight
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are not recommended for BPD because they undermine distress tolerance skills, risk disinhibition and dependence, and contradict the structured care plan in place. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Borderline Personality Disorder 2012)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Borderline Personality Disorder 2012

options.text: Rewrite the discharge plan now to grant unescorted leave tomorrow morning and document her request as informed choice
options.rationale: Unilateral overnight plan changes undermine team consistency, reinforce splitting and bypass the multidisciplinary process required for high-quality BPD care. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Borderline Personality Disorder 2012)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Borderline Personality Disorder 2012

options.text: Empathically validate her distress, decline plan changes overnight and hand over to the day team for the next morning
options.rationale: Consistency with the existing DBT-informed plan, validation without capitulation and structured handover maintain therapeutic boundaries and reduce reinforcement of contingency-driven self-harm threats. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Borderline Personality Disorder 2012)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Borderline Personality Disorder 2012

options.text: Confront her about the manipulative nature of her request and document that her behaviour is attention-seeking only
options.rationale: Pejorative labels such as manipulative or attention-seeking are explicitly discouraged in BPD care because they damage therapeutic alliance and are clinically inaccurate. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Borderline Personality Disorder 2012)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Borderline Personality Disorder 2012

explanation.summary: BPD care requires consistency, validation and team cohesion. Splitting behaviours, where one clinician is idealised and another devalued, are managed by uniform adherence to the agreed care plan rather than unilateral overnight changes. Benzodiazepines and pejorative labels both worsen outcomes. The NHMRC clinical practice guideline emphasises empathic limit-setting, validation of distress, and handover to the multidisciplinary day team for plan changes.
key_points: Maintain plan consistency overnight; defer changes to the multidisciplinary day team
Avoid benzodiazepines in BPD
Pejorative labels worsen outcomes and are clinically inaccurate
Mental Health Act not for contingency-driven threats without acute illness change
explanation.pearls: When a BPD inpatient asks for an overnight plan change while flattering the on-call doctor and threatening self-harm, respond with validation plus a clear statement that the existing plan remains until the multidisciplinary team reviews in the morning, and chart no new benzodiazepine.

sources.label: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Borderline Personality Disorder 2012
Project Air Strategy for Personality Disorders

stem:
A 48-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been on lithium 750 mg nocte for 14 years with stable mood and serum levels around 0.7 mmol/L. At routine review she reports increasing thirst over the last four months with polyuria of around 4 litres per day, nocturia twice nightly, and unchanged mood. She is on no other relevant medications, drinks one to two cups of tea per day, and has no recent infection.

Her GP has been monitoring six-monthly bloods. Today bloods show stable lithium level, mildly reduced eGFR from her baseline, normal calcium and glucose, and notably dilute urine.

data_table.Examination: Mucous membranes moist; no signs of dehydration; mood euthymic
data_table.Investigations: Li 0.7 mmol/L; Na 142, K 4.1, eGFR 58 (baseline 72) mL/min/1.73m2; corrected Ca 2.30, glucose 5.2, urine osm 180 mOsm/kg, serum osm 295
data_table.Medications: Lithium 750 mg nocte; quetiapine 100 mg nocte

lead_in: What is the most likely cause of her polyuria and best initial action?

options.text: Central diabetes insipidus from undiagnosed pituitary lesion; arrange pituitary MRI and copeptin level before any treatment
options.rationale: Central diabetes insipidus is far less likely than lithium-induced nephrogenic in a long-term lithium user with no other pituitary features; pituitary MRI is not the first investigation here. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders 2020)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders 2020

options.text: Primary polydipsia from psychotropic medication dry mouth; advise fluid restriction to 1.5 litres daily and review in eight weeks
options.rationale: Dilute urine with normal serum osmolality and appropriate intake makes primary polydipsia less likely; fluid restriction in nephrogenic diabetes insipidus risks dangerous hypernatraemia. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders 2020)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders 2020

options.text: New-onset type 2 diabetes mellitus from quetiapine; arrange fasting glucose, HbA1c and lipid panel before the next review
options.rationale: Glucose is normal and the urine osmolality pattern is wrong for osmotic diuresis, which would yield concentrated rather than dilute urine. (Source: Australian Medicines Handbook 2025: quetiapine)
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook 2025: quetiapine

options.text: Lithium-induced hypercalcaemia driving polyuria; arrange parathyroid hormone level and outpatient endocrinology referral
options.rationale: Lithium can cause hyperparathyroidism with hypercalcaemic polyuria but this patient has normal corrected calcium, making this diagnosis unlikely as the primary cause. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders 2020)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders 2020

options.text: Lithium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus; arrange psychiatrist review for lithium dose reduction or switch to alternative mood stabiliser
options.rationale: Dilute urine with appropriate serum osmolality, polyuria and long-term lithium use is classic nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, an aquaporin-2 channelopathy from chronic lithium exposure; specialist review for dose minimisation or switch with renal preservation is the priority. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders 2020)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders 2020

explanation.summary: Chronic lithium exposure causes nephrogenic diabetes insipidus by downregulating collecting duct aquaporin-2 channels, producing polyuria with inappropriately dilute urine despite normal or high serum osmolality. Long-term lithium also produces chronic interstitial nephropathy with gradual eGFR decline. Management involves liaison with the treating psychiatrist about minimising lithium dose, switching to an alternative mood stabiliser where appropriate, and avoiding nephrotoxic combinations. Amiloride may be added under specialist care in selected patients.
key_points: Long-term lithium causes nephrogenic diabetes insipidus via aquaporin-2 downregulation
Lithium nephropathy produces gradual eGFR decline over years
Manage by minimising lithium dose or switching mood stabiliser
Avoid fluid restriction in nephrogenic diabetes insipidus
explanation.pearls: When long-term lithium patients develop polyuria with urine osmolality <300 mOsm/kg and appropriate serum osmolality, attribute it to nephrogenic diabetes insipidus and arrange psychiatrist-led mood stabiliser review rather than fluid restricting or chasing alternative endocrine causes first.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders 2020

stem:
A 29-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder presents to your community mental health clinic on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily prescribed by a previous psychiatrist three years ago, with stable mood and no current relationship. She tells you she has met a new partner and is considering trying for pregnancy in the next twelve months. She has not had pre-pregnancy counselling about her current mood stabiliser.

She has private health insurance and good engagement with services. She has no history of treatment-resistant illness and previously responded well to lithium during a first episode of mania six years ago.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic mood; no current psychotic, depressive or manic features
data_table.Investigations: Valproate level 520 micromol/L; LFTs normal; FBC normal; eGFR 88

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Plan a structured cross-taper from valproate back to lithium, with reproductive counselling and adherent contraception during transition
options.rationale: Valproate use in women of reproductive potential requires the Pregnancy Prevention Program plus active planning to switch to safer alternatives such as lithium when clinically appropriate; lithium previously worked and carries a lower teratogenic risk profile with appropriate monitoring. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program 2023)
source_refs: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program 2023

options.text: Continue sodium valproate at current dose and add high-dose folate 5 mg daily pre-conception to mitigate neural tube defect risk
options.rationale: Folate does not abolish valproate teratogenic and neurodevelopmental risks; valproate carries the highest teratogenic and neurodevelopmental risk profile among mood stabilisers and should be avoided in women planning pregnancy. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program 2023)
source_refs: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program 2023

options.text: Halve valproate dose to 500 mg daily as low-dose exposure abolishes teratogenic and neurodevelopmental concerns in pregnancy planning
options.rationale: Lower-dose valproate reduces but does not abolish teratogenic and neurodevelopmental risks; there is no safe dose during conception planning in women of reproductive potential. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program 2023)
source_refs: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program 2023

options.text: Discontinue valproate immediately today without alternative mood stabiliser cover and review again at the next routine outpatient slot
options.rationale: Abrupt discontinuation of valproate without cover risks destabilisation and a manic relapse; planned cross-taper to an appropriate alternative is required rather than abrupt stop. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders 2020)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders 2020

options.text: Switch valproate to carbamazepine as a safer pregnancy option given carbamazepine has no major congenital malformation signal currently
options.rationale: Carbamazepine is also teratogenic with neural tube defect and craniofacial signals and is not a preferred substitute for valproate in pregnancy planning. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program 2023)
source_refs: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program 2023

explanation.summary: Sodium valproate carries the highest teratogenic burden among mood stabilisers with approximately 10% major congenital malformation risk and around 30 to 40% neurodevelopmental disorder risk in exposed offspring. The TGA Pregnancy Prevention Program mandates structured counselling, contraception and annual review in any woman of reproductive potential prescribed valproate, plus active planning to switch to safer alternatives when feasible. Lithium remains an appropriate first-line mood stabiliser in pre-conception planning when historically effective, with structured monitoring of levels in pregnancy. Lamotrigine is another option for predominantly depressive bipolar illness.
key_points: Valproate carries the highest teratogenic and neurodevelopmental risk of mood stabilisers
There is no safe valproate dose in pre-conception planning
Lithium with monitoring is an appropriate alternative when historically effective
Abrupt discontinuation risks manic relapse; plan cross-taper
explanation.pearls: For a woman of reproductive potential on valproate planning pregnancy within 12 months, initiate a structured cross-taper to lithium or lamotrigine over weeks with continued contraception throughout, rather than dose-reducing valproate or relying on folate supplementation.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders 2020

stem:
You are the psychiatry registrar reviewing a 34-year-old woman admitted to the medical ward two days ago with reduced oral intake and minimal speech for one week. She has a history of recurrent severe major depressive disorder. On examination she lies in bed, makes no spontaneous movement, holds an arm in any position you place it for several minutes, and is mute.

She has resistance to passive limb movement that increases with examiner force. Bloods including creatine kinase are mildly elevated, infection screen is negative and CT brain is normal. The medical team has been giving small-volume nasogastric feeds.

data_table.Examination: Mutism, waxy flexibility (catalepsy), negativism, posturing; Bush-Francis 14
data_table.Investigations: CK 380 U/L (mildly elevated); WCC 7.2; CRP 6; CT brain normal; UDS negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Commence olanzapine 5 mg orally daily for presumed catatonic schizophrenia and reassess response after seven days of treatment
options.rationale: Antipsychotic monotherapy in catatonia risks worsening catatonia and precipitating neuroleptic malignant syndrome; first-line therapy is benzodiazepine, with antipsychotic added cautiously later if a psychotic disorder is established. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Catatonia)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Catatonia

options.text: Administer a single dose of lorazepam 1 to 2 mg parenterally and reassess catatonic signs within 10 to 30 minutes after administration
options.rationale: Bush-Francis score with multiple cardinal features mandates a lorazepam challenge; meaningful reduction in catatonic signs within 30 minutes confirms the diagnosis and guides scheduled benzodiazepine therapy. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Catatonia)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Catatonia

options.text: Increase her existing sertraline back to 100 mg daily for the presumed underlying severe depressive episode currently driving symptoms
options.rationale: SSRI titration does not address catatonia in the short term and delays the lorazepam challenge that is the standard of care for diagnostic confirmation and rapid relief. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Catatonia)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Catatonia

options.text: Arrange MRI brain with contrast and lumbar puncture before any pharmacological intervention to exclude an autoimmune encephalitic process
options.rationale: Autoimmune encephalitis should be considered if response is poor, but it does not delay an initial lorazepam challenge, which is diagnostically informative and rapidly reversible. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Catatonia)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Catatonia

options.text: Refer urgently for inpatient electroconvulsive therapy without any preceding benzodiazepine trial regardless of the catatonia subtype
options.rationale: ECT is highly effective for catatonia but is typically used second-line after benzodiazepine failure or first-line only in malignant catatonia with autonomic instability. (Source: RANZCP Position Statement on ECT)
source_refs: RANZCP Position Statement on ECT

explanation.summary: Catatonia is recognised by Bush-Francis signs including mutism, waxy flexibility, negativism and posturing, and may complicate severe mood disorders, psychosis, autoimmune encephalitis, or medical illness. First-line management is a lorazepam challenge of 1 to 2 mg parenterally with reassessment within 30 minutes. A positive response supports diagnosis and scheduled benzodiazepine therapy. Antipsychotic monotherapy risks worsening catatonia and triggering neuroleptic malignant syndrome. ECT is highly effective second-line therapy, particularly for malignant catatonia.
key_points: Bush-Francis assesses cardinal catatonic signs at the bedside
Lorazepam challenge 1 to 2 mg parenterally is first-line diagnostically and therapeutically
Avoid antipsychotic monotherapy because of NMS risk
ECT is highly effective second-line therapy
explanation.pearls: When a depressed inpatient is mute with waxy flexibility, give a single parenteral lorazepam 1 to 2 mg and reassess Bush-Francis at 30 minutes; a substantial reduction confirms catatonia and warrants scheduled lorazepam 6 to 24 mg daily titrated to response.

stem: A 38-year-old woman presents to your emergency department with new-onset right lower limb weakness developing over three hours, with no antecedent trauma. She works as a registered nurse and is currently on long-term workers compensation for a back injury. On examination she demonstrates 1/5 strength on direct testing of right hip flexion but contralateral hip extension produces strong synergistic activation in the right hip extensors that is inconsistent with the deficit, and her Hoover sign is positive. Sensation is intact. Reflexes and tone are normal. MRI brain and spine are unremarkable.

data_table.Examination: 1/5 right hip flexion; positive Hoover sign; normal tone and reflexes
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain and lumbar spine: no acute pathology; routine bloods normal
data_table.Medications: Paracetamol PRN for chronic back pain

lead_in: Which diagnosis is most consistent with the examination findings?

options.text: Factitious disorder imposed on self given her medical knowledge and unconscious sick role pursuit without external incentive present
options.rationale: Factitious disorder requires evidence of intentional production of signs for primary internal sick-role psychological gain; a single examination finding does not establish this and conversion remains the better fit. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Functional Neurological Disorder)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Functional Neurological Disorder

options.text: Malingering given workers compensation context with deliberate symptom fabrication for external financial secondary gain on history
options.rationale: Malingering requires evidence of conscious feigning for external incentive; positive Hoover sign demonstrates internal inconsistency but does not establish conscious motivation, and conversion is more common in clinical practice. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Functional Neurological Disorder)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Functional Neurological Disorder

options.text: Functional neurological disorder (conversion type) based on positive Hoover sign and clinical inconsistency on internal validation
options.rationale: Functional neurological disorder is now a rule-in diagnosis based on positive clinical signs such as Hoover sign demonstrating internal inconsistency between voluntary and automatic movement, not a diagnosis of exclusion. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Functional Neurological Disorder)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Functional Neurological Disorder

options.text: Acute ischaemic stroke despite a normal MRI given limb weakness can precede imaging changes within early acute ischaemic stroke windows
options.rationale: MRI diffusion-weighted imaging is highly sensitive for acute ischaemia within hours; Hoover sign positive plus normal MRI argues strongly against stroke as the explanation. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Acute Ischaemic Stroke)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Acute Ischaemic Stroke

options.text: Subacute combined degeneration of the cord from undiagnosed pernicious anaemia with vibration loss and ataxia in lower limbs predominantly
options.rationale: Subacute combined degeneration presents with dorsal column and corticospinal tract signs typically symmetrical with reduced vibration and proprioception; this presentation lacks those features and shows a positive Hoover sign. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Vitamin B12 Deficiency)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Vitamin B12 Deficiency

explanation.summary: Functional neurological disorder is diagnosed by positive clinical signs of internal inconsistency, not by exclusion. Hoover sign is highly specific: ipsilateral hip extension is weak on direct testing but normal when contralateral hip flexion is tested against resistance. The Australian and international shift positions FND as a positive rule-in diagnosis to be communicated openly and treated with FND-specific physiotherapy and psychological therapies. Malingering and factitious disorder require evidence of intentional production for external or internal incentives respectively, which Hoover sign alone cannot establish.
key_points: FND is a rule-in diagnosis from positive clinical signs
Hoover sign demonstrates internal inconsistency between voluntary and automatic movement
Malingering needs evidence of conscious feigning for external gain
Factitious disorder needs evidence of intentional production for internal sick-role gain
explanation.pearls: When weakness is associated with a positive Hoover sign and normal imaging, communicate functional neurological disorder as a rule-in diagnosis and refer for FND-specific physiotherapy plus psychology rather than ordering further structural investigations or labelling the presentation as malingering.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Functional Neurological Disorder
BMJ Best Practice: Acute Ischaemic Stroke
BMJ Best Practice: Vitamin B12 Deficiency

stem: A 32-year-old man is brought to your emergency department by friends after 14 hours of severe agitation, tremor, profuse sweating and visual hallucinations. He attends regularly at dance events on weekends and his friends report he has been dosing 1,4-butanediol (a GHB precursor) every 2 hours around the clock for the past three months to maintain function. He last dosed approximately 18 hours ago. He drinks alcohol minimally and denies opioid use. His urine drug screen detects no opioids, benzodiazepines, amphetamines or alcohol on standard panel testing.

data_table.Examination: Tremor, diaphoresis, mydriasis, visual hallucinations; oriented; CIWA-Ar 24
data_table.Investigations: UDS negative on standard panel; FBC and UEC normal; CK 240

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological management?

options.text: Discharge home with referral to an outpatient drug and alcohol service for community-based GHB withdrawal management within seven working days
options.rationale: Severe GHB withdrawal with autonomic instability and hallucinations is life-threatening and requires inpatient monitored care, not community management. (Source: NSW Health GHB Use and Withdrawal Management Guidelines 2018)
source_refs: NSW Health GHB Use and Withdrawal Management Guidelines 2018

options.text: Administer naloxone 0.4 mg intravenously immediately for presumed concealed opioid withdrawal as the underlying cause of his autonomic instability
options.rationale: Naloxone treats opioid intoxication not withdrawal, and the clinical and history pattern strongly indicates GHB-precursor withdrawal rather than opioid involvement. (Source: NSW Health GHB Use and Withdrawal Management Guidelines 2018)
source_refs: NSW Health GHB Use and Withdrawal Management Guidelines 2018

options.text: Use intravenous haloperidol as primary pharmacotherapy for agitation given his prominent visual hallucinations on initial presentation today
options.rationale: Antipsychotics do not address the underlying GABAergic withdrawal and risk lowering seizure threshold; benzodiazepines are first-line for symptom and seizure control. (Source: NSW Health GHB Use and Withdrawal Management Guidelines 2018)
source_refs: NSW Health GHB Use and Withdrawal Management Guidelines 2018

options.text: Commence high-dose oral diazepam titrated to symptom control with parenteral midazolam available for breakthrough agitation in resuscitation
options.rationale: GHB and precursor withdrawal is GABAergic-mediated, mirrors severe alcohol or benzodiazepine withdrawal and characteristically requires very high benzodiazepine doses with low threshold for parenteral escalation in a monitored setting. (Source: NSW Health GHB Use and Withdrawal Management Guidelines 2018)
source_refs: NSW Health GHB Use and Withdrawal Management Guidelines 2018

options.text: Administer intravenous flumazenil 0.2 mg as a diagnostic test for benzodiazepine intoxication given his presentation of prominent autonomic features
options.rationale: Flumazenil risks precipitating seizures, particularly in GABAergic withdrawal states, and has no role in suspected GHB withdrawal management. (Source: Australian Medicines Handbook 2025: flumazenil)
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook 2025: flumazenil

explanation.summary: GHB and its precursors (1,4-butanediol, GBL) produce a severe GABAergic withdrawal syndrome clinically resembling delirium tremens, often with autonomic instability, hallucinations and seizure risk. Standard urine drug screens do not detect GHB. Patients who dose around the clock are at highest risk. Management requires inpatient monitored care with high-dose benzodiazepine titration; phenobarbitone or propofol may be used as adjuncts in severe cases.
key_points: GHB withdrawal is GABAergic and mimics severe alcohol withdrawal
Around-the-clock dosing is a high-risk pattern
Standard UDS does not detect GHB
Benzodiazepines are first-line; very high doses are often required
explanation.pearls: When a regular GHB or 1,4-butanediol user presents with CIWA-Ar over 15 within 24 hours of last dose, admit to a monitored bed and titrate diazepam in 10 to 20 mg increments until controlled rather than discharging or relying on antipsychotic monotherapy.

sources.label: NSW Health GHB Use and Withdrawal Management Guidelines 2018

stem:
A 27-year-old man is admitted to your acute mental health unit with a four-day history of decreased sleep, grandiose religious beliefs and pressured speech. He has used crystal methamphetamine intermittently on weekends for two years and his last reported use was nine days ago. His urine drug screen is negative for amphetamines today and was negative on admission three days ago.

Hair testing is unavailable in your service area. He has no first-degree family history of bipolar disorder. After three days of olanzapine 10 mg nocte and observed abstinence on the ward, he remains overtly manic with grandiosity and minimal sleep.

data_table.Examination: Pressured speech, flight of ideas, grandiose delusions, minimal need for sleep
data_table.Investigations: UDS amphetamines negative on admission and day 3; TSH 1.4; LFTs normal
data_table.Medications: Olanzapine 10 mg nocte for 3 days; no benzodiazepines

lead_in: Which diagnosis and management combination best fits the clinical course on day 4 of inpatient stay?

options.text: Methamphetamine-induced psychotic disorder with mood features; continue olanzapine, defer mood stabiliser pending one month of verified observed abstinence
options.rationale: Persistence of full manic syndrome beyond one week of observed verified abstinence with negative serial urine drug screens favours primary bipolar I rather than substance-induced disorder; methamphetamine clearance is rapid, and continuing without a mood stabiliser risks under-treatment. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders 2020)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders 2020

options.text: Methamphetamine intoxication despite negative urine drug screening; observe further without any pharmacological intensification of treatment for a further 48 hours
options.rationale: Methamphetamine has a half-life of around 10 hours and intoxication does not persist for nine days post use; serial negative urine drug screens and clinical persistence make ongoing intoxication implausible. (Source: Australian Medicines Handbook 2025: amphetamines)
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook 2025: amphetamines

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder bipolar type; continue olanzapine, plan early discharge to outpatient follow-up without inpatient mood stabiliser initiation today
options.rationale: Schizoaffective disorder requires at least two weeks of psychosis without prominent mood symptoms in the lifetime course; this single episode of mania with mood-congruent psychosis does not yet satisfy schizoaffective criteria. (Source: DSM-5-TR Schizoaffective Disorder Criteria)
source_refs: DSM-5-TR Schizoaffective Disorder Criteria

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder with marked stressor of recent methamphetamine cessation; taper olanzapine over the next week prior to outpatient psychology referral
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder lasts less than one month and is characterised by psychotic rather than full manic symptoms; this presentation meets manic episode criteria and warrants mood stabiliser cover. (Source: DSM-5-TR Brief Psychotic Disorder Criteria)
source_refs: DSM-5-TR Brief Psychotic Disorder Criteria

options.text: Bipolar I disorder current episode manic; add lithium 750 mg nocte as a mood stabiliser to current olanzapine and target serum level 0.6 to 1.0 mmol/L
options.rationale: Manic syndrome persisting beyond expected methamphetamine and metabolite clearance with multiple negative urine drug screens during a verified inpatient abstinence period meets bipolar I criteria, and lithium combined with antipsychotic is appropriate first-line care for acute mania. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders 2020)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders 2020

explanation.summary: Methamphetamine intoxication clears within 24 to 48 hours and methamphetamine-induced symptoms typically resolve within days to weeks of abstinence. Persistence of a full manic syndrome despite verified observed abstinence and serial negative urine drug screens (typically detecting use within 1 to 3 days) shifts the diagnosis toward primary bipolar I disorder. Substance-use comorbidity is common, but the diagnostic and treatment implications differ. Combination antipsychotic plus lithium is first-line treatment for acute mania in bipolar I.
key_points: Methamphetamine clears within hours; intoxication does not persist for days
Symptom persistence beyond a verified abstinence period favours primary bipolar I
Serial urine drug screens detect amphetamines within 1 to 3 days of use
Combination antipsychotic plus lithium is first-line for acute mania
explanation.pearls: When a patient with prior methamphetamine use shows full mania beyond seven days of observed inpatient abstinence with serial negative urine drug screens, treat as bipolar I disorder current episode manic and add lithium targeting 0.6 to 1.0 mmol/L to the antipsychotic rather than relying on observation alone.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders 2020
